metadata.career_relevance: Microservices is the dominant architecture at every large Indian tech company вҖ” Flipkart, Swiggy, Zomato, Zerodha, PhonePe, Razorpay all run hundreds of microservices. Senior engineers design them. Mid-level engineers work within them daily. Even as a junior developer, understanding the architecture of the system you're working in is expected. After this module, you can contribute to and reason about microservices systems вҖ” a key differentiator in interviews.

hook.real_world_problem:
The Flipkart monolith of 2010: one giant Spring application with inventory, orders, payments, search, recommendations, user accounts, notifications вҖ” all in the same codebase. Every deploy required deploying everything. One bug in recommendations could crash payments. Scaling search required scaling everything. 500 developers trying to merge code into the same repo daily.

**The microservices migration (2012-2015):**
```
BEFORE (monolith):
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                   flipkart-app (1 deployable)         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  [Orders] [Inventory] [Search] [Payment] [Users] ... в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
     вҶ“ one bug crashes everything
     вҶ“ can't scale search independently
     вҶ“ 500 devs fighting over one codebase

AFTER (microservices):
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Order   в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Inventory в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Search  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Payment в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Service  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Service  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  Service в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Service в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ (50 devs)в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (30 devs) в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (80 devs)в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ(40 devs)в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
     вҶ“ each team deploys independently
     вҶ“ scale only what's under load
     вҶ“ Search bug doesn't affect Payment
```

Each service is a separate Spring Boot application. They communicate over HTTP (REST). Each has its own database. Teams are independent. This is microservices вҖ” and it's how India's major tech companies are built.
hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll understand microservices vs monolith tradeoffs, how to decompose a system by domain, how services communicate with HTTP, and how to make inter-service HTTP calls from a Spring Boot service using RestTemplate.

concept_explanation.monolith_vs_microservices:
**Monolith vs Microservices вҖ” the core distinction:**

```
в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җ
   MONOLITH                    MICROSERVICES
в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җ
 One deployable unit           Many small deployables
 One database                  One database per service
 One codebase                  One repo per service (usually)
 Scale everything together      Scale individual services
 Simple local method calls      Network calls (HTTP/messaging)
 Easy to develop initially      Complex distributed system
 Hard to scale teams            Teams own their service
 Deploy all or nothing          Deploy services independently
 One technology stack           Different stack per service
в•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җ

When to use microservices:
вң“ Large team (> 20-30 developers)
вң“ Different scaling needs per component
вң“ Independent release cadence needed
вң“ Long-term product with clear domain boundaries

When NOT to use:
вң— Small team (< 10 developers)
вң— Early-stage product (domains not yet clear)
вң— Simple CRUD application
вң— When operational complexity would dominate
```
concept_explanation.service_decomposition:
**How to decompose: Domain-Driven Design (DDD):**

For a Swiggy-style food delivery app:

```
Domain Boundaries вҶ’ Services:

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ User Domain      вҶ’ user-service                    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (register, login, profile, addresses)             в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Restaurant Domain вҶ’ restaurant-service             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (menus, availability, ratings)                    в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Order Domain     вҶ’ order-service                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (cart, checkout, order lifecycle)                 в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Payment Domain   вҶ’ payment-service                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (payment processing, refunds, history)            в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Delivery Domain  вҶ’ delivery-service                в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (driver assignment, tracking, ETA)                в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Notification Domain вҶ’ notification-service         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (SMS, email, push notifications)                  в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Each service:
- Owns its own database
- Has its own team
- Deploys on its own schedule
- Exposes REST APIs for other services
```

**Key decomposition principles:**
1. Services own their data вҖ” no shared database
2. Communicate via APIs (HTTP or events), not shared code
3. Single responsibility вҖ” one domain per service
4. Independently deployable
concept_explanation.inter_service_communication:
**How services talk to each other:**

**Synchronous (request-response):**
```
Order Service вҶ’ HTTP GET вҶ’ Inventory Service вҶ’ 200 {inStock: true}
                    вҶ‘ waits for response (blocking)
```

**Asynchronous (events):**
```
Order Service вҶ’ publishes 'order.created' event вҶ’ message queue
                    вҶ‘ doesn't wait
Inventory Service reads 'order.created' вҶ’ decrements stock
Notification Service reads 'order.created' вҶ’ sends SMS
```

**Which to use:**
- Synchronous HTTP: when you need the response to continue (check inventory before confirming order)
- Asynchronous events: when you can proceed without waiting (send notification after order is placed)

This topic covers synchronous HTTP. Topic 15.4 covers async with events.
concept_explanation.data_isolation:
**Each service owns its own data вҖ” the most important rule:**

```
вң… CORRECT вҖ” data isolation:
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   order-service      в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  inventory-service    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ       в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   в”Ӯorders_db в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ   в”Ӯinventory в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ(PostgreSQL)      в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ  _db     в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ       в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ       в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
  Communicates via REST API only

вқҢ WRONG вҖ” shared database:
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   order-service      в”Ӯ    в”Ӯ  inventory-service    в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
           в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                   в”Ӯshared_dbв”Ӯ  вҶҗ antipattern
                   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
  Both services can now see each other's tables.
  Schema changes in one break the other.
  Services are tightly coupled вҖ” defeats the purpose.
```

When order-service needs inventory data: it calls the inventory-service REST API. It never reaches directly into the inventory database.

visual_aid.description: Three panels: (1) Swiggy-style system diagram: Mobile App вҶ’ API Gateway вҶ’ fan-out to User Service, Restaurant Service, Order Service, Payment Service, Delivery Service, Notification Service. Each service has its own database cylinder. Services communicate to each other with HTTP arrows (OrderвҶ’Inventory, OrderвҶ’Payment, OrderвҶ’Notification). (2) HTTP inter-service call flow: OrderService.placeOrder() вҶ’ RestTemplate.getForObject('http://inventory-service/...') вҶ’ HTTP GET вҶ’ InventoryController.checkStock() вҶ’ 200 {stock:50} вҶ’ back to OrderService. (3) Monolith vs microservices scaling: monolith needs to scale all components; microservices shows only Search Service scaled (3 instances) while others stay at 1, demonstrating independent scaling.
visual_aid.alt_text: Swiggy-style microservices diagram, HTTP inter-service call sequence, and independent scaling comparison

worked_example.scenario: Build two collaborating Spring Boot microservices: product-service (manages products) and order-service (places orders, calling product-service to check availability). Each runs on a different port, has its own H2 database.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Build:
1. `product-service` on port 8081: @Entity Product, GET /api/products/{id}, GET /api/products/{id}/availability
2. `order-service` on port 8082: calls product-service via RestTemplate before placing order
3. If product-service returns unavailable вҶ’ order-service returns 409
4. Each service has its own H2 database (no shared state)

step_by_step_solution.action: product-service вҖ” standalone Spring Boot app
order-service вҖ” calls product-service via RestTemplate

things_to_notice: Two completely separate Spring Boot applications вҖ” separate pom.xml, separate main class, separate application.properties, different ports. They don't share any code or database. This is the key difference from a modular monolith.

The product-service URL is hardcoded as `http://localhost:8081`. This works locally but fails in production where services might move or have multiple instances. Topic 15.2 introduces service discovery (Eureka) to solve this вҖ” services register themselves and are discovered by name, not hardcoded URL.

When product-service is down, the RestTemplate.getForObject() call throws ResourceAccessException. The order-service has no fallback вҖ” the entire placeOrder() fails. Topic 15.3 covers circuit breakers (Resilience4j) which handle this gracefully.

Each service has its own H2 database. The order-service only stores orders вҖ” it never directly reads or writes to product-service's tables. It gets product info through the API. This is data isolation вҖ” each service's database schema is its private implementation detail.

RestTemplate makes a synchronous HTTP call вҖ” the order-service thread blocks until product-service responds. For high-throughput services, WebClient (reactive) or Feign with async support is preferred. For this pattern: if product-service is slow, order-service is slow.

industry_examples.use_case: When a user places an order: (1) order-service creates the order, (2) calls restaurant-service to confirm the restaurant is open, (3) calls payment-service to charge the user, (4) calls delivery-service to assign a driver, (5) publishes 'order.placed' event вҖ” notification-service sends SMS/push. Steps 1-4 are synchronous (need confirmation). Step 5 is asynchronous (notification can be slightly delayed). The same architecture you built вҖ” at Swiggy scale.

When a user places a trade: order-service calls account-service (margin check), calls market-service (price fetch), calls risk-service (limit check). All synchronous вҖ” you need all answers before executing. Then calls audit-service (async вҖ” record for compliance). Then notification-service (async вҖ” 'Your order for TCS has been placed'). This layering of sync + async is the standard microservices pattern.

Most companies don't start with microservices вҖ” they start as a monolith and extract services as the team and system grows. The 'strangler fig' pattern: add a new feature as a standalone microservice instead of adding to the monolith. Over time, extract existing monolith features into services. Flipkart, Swiggy, Zomato all did this. You start with a Spring Boot monolith (Modules 13-14), then split it (Module 15).

applications.interview_relevance:
Microservices interview questions вҖ” must know:

- **'What is a microservice?'** вҶ’ A small, independently deployable service that owns one domain (bounded context), has its own database, and communicates with other services via APIs.
- **'What are the advantages of microservices?'** вҶ’ Independent deployment, independent scaling, team autonomy, technology flexibility, fault isolation.
- **'What are the disadvantages?'** вҶ’ Network latency, distributed systems complexity, data consistency challenges, operational overhead, harder to debug.
- **'How do microservices communicate?'** вҶ’ Synchronous: REST (HTTP) or gRPC. Asynchronous: message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ).
- **'What is data isolation?'** вҶ’ Each service has its own database. No service directly accesses another service's database вҖ” only via APIs.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily microservices development workflow:

- Work on one service at a time вҖ” your team's service
- Run dependent services locally (or use stubs/mocks)
- Make REST API calls to other services
- Check API contracts (Swagger/OpenAPI docs)
- Deploy your service independently when ready
- Monitor with distributed tracing to track requests across services

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Having two services connect to the same database вҖ” defeats the purpose of microservices.
common_gaps.remediation: Each service must own its own database. If service A needs data that belongs to service B, it calls service B's REST API. Service A never directly queries service B's database tables. This is the most important microservices rule.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Both services use the same spring.datasource.url

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Making every class a service вҖ” 'nanoservices' with 50 services for a 5-person team.
common_gaps.remediation: Decompose by domain (bounded context), not by class. A 'Product' service and a 'ProductImage' service for a small team is too granular. Rule of thumb: 5-15 services for a team of 50 engineers. Services should be owned by one team and have meaningful, testable domain logic.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: More services than developers; trivial single-endpoint services

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using synchronous REST for all inter-service communication, even for non-critical notifications.
common_gaps.remediation: If service A calls service B synchronously but doesn't need the response to proceed, that's a candidate for async messaging. Sending a welcome email after user registration doesn't need to block the registration response. Use async (Kafka/RabbitMQ) for notifications, logging, analytics вҖ” anything where eventual consistency is acceptable.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Response time = sum of all downstream service response times

key_takeaways: Microservice: small, independently deployable, owns one domain, has its own database
Key benefits: independent deployment, scaling, team autonomy, fault isolation
Key costs: network latency, distributed complexity, operational overhead
Decompose by domain (DDD bounded context), not by technical layer
Each service owns its own database вҖ” no shared database tables
Communication: synchronous REST for immediate responses, async events for non-critical
RestTemplate: makes HTTP calls to other services (getForObject, postForObject)
Hardcoded URLs вҶ’ use service discovery (Eureka) to find services by name
No response handling вҶ’ use circuit breakers (Resilience4j) for fault tolerance
Module 15 progression: RestTemplate вҶ’ Feign вҶ’ Eureka вҶ’ Resilience4j вҶ’ Tracing

self_check_questions.question: An order-service needs the current price of a product before creating an order. Should this call be synchronous or asynchronous? Explain.
self_check_questions.answer: Synchronous. The order-service cannot proceed with creating the order without knowing the product price вҖ” it needs the response to calculate the order total and confirm the order. Synchronous REST call to product-service: `GET /api/products/{id}/price` вҶ’ 200 {price: 3890}. The order-service blocks until product-service responds. Async would not work here because the order-service needs the price BEFORE it can save the order.

self_check_questions.question: You want to notify customers via SMS when their order ships. Should the order-service call notification-service synchronously or asynchronously? Why?
self_check_questions.answer: Asynchronously. The order-service doesn't need the SMS to be sent before it can mark the order as shipped and respond to the user. If notification-service is slow or down, the shipment confirmation shouldn't fail. Async: order-service publishes an 'order.shipped' event to a queue. Notification-service reads from the queue and sends SMS вҖ” maybe 1-2 seconds later. Eventual consistency is acceptable for notifications. Using synchronous REST here would make the shipment confirmation as slow as the SMS delivery.

self_check_questions.question: Order-service and inventory-service both need to store product IDs. Should they share a products database table?
self_check_questions.answer: No. Sharing a database table violates data isolation вҖ” the most important microservices principle. Each service should have its own copy of the data it needs: order-service stores productId (a foreign key by convention, not enforced by DB), inventory-service manages the full product inventory record. If order-service needs product details, it calls product-service via REST API. The services may both store a productId, but they never share the same database schema. Schema changes in one service would break the other вҖ” exactly what microservices isolation prevents.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **Feign Client** вҖ” RestTemplate works but requires boilerplate URL building, response handling, and error management. Feign Client is a declarative HTTP client: you declare a Java interface with Spring MVC annotations and Feign generates the implementation. `@FeignClient('inventory-service')` + `@GetMapping('/api/inventory/{id}')` = a method that calls inventory-service as if it were a local call. Cleaner, type-safe, and integrates with Eureka service discovery automatically.
